data.title: Energy Up as Ukraine, Gaza Risks Push Up Oil - Energy Roundup
data.description:
Shares of energy companies rose as uncertainty about the Ukraine and Gaza conflicts drove up oil futures.

Oil futures rose 5% in New York this week to close at $65.72 a barrel.

Ukraine has recently increased attacks on Russian refineries and energy infrastructure.

Under a White House plan that is being proposed to Arab and Israeli leaders, former British Prime Minister Tony Blair, 72, would serve as interim administrator of Gaza, overseeing a body known as the Gaza International Transition Authority, or GITA.

Advisory firm Institutional Shareholder Services is giving a lukewarm endorsement to Cenovus Energy's CVE-T $7-billion bid for MEG Energy Corp.

Italy's competition authority imposed more than $1.09 billion of fines on local energy producer Eni and five other oil companies following an investigation into an alleged cartel approach to setting fuel prices.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

Big 5 Sporting Goods Corp :

  • BIG 5 SPORTING GOODS CORPORATION STOCKHOLDERS APPROVE ACQUISITION BY WORLDWIDE SPORTS GROUP HOLDINGS LLC

data.title: Utilities Up as Treasury Yields Retreat - Utilities Roundup
data.description:
Shares of power producers rose after Treasury yields fell in response to inflation data.

Private fund manager EIG Global Energy Partners has been investing at a fast pace across the energy industry while making a particular effort to expand MidOcean Energy, its liquefied natural gas business, including a multibillion-dollar commitment to a Gulf Coast LNG project, its first in the U.S.

Real estate investment trust Fermi is planning to raise billions of dollars in an initial public offering that will help it fund development of a nuclear power plant and other generating facilities.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Tech Up Amid Deal Activity - Tech Roundup
data.description:
Shares of technology companies rose amid deal activity.

Electronic Arts rose by almost 15% after the videogame maker reportedly neared a deal with private equity firms for a roughly $50 billion leveraged buyout, which could be the largest in history.

Peers such as TakeTwo Interactive rose in sympathy.

Shares of digital-advertising technology firm AppLovin rallied after analysts at Wall Street brokerages Piper Sandler and UBS raised their price targets on its shares, citing growth potential.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Materials Up After Inflation Data - Materials Roundup
data.description:
Producers of metals and other raw materials rose after inflation data.

The personal consumption expenditures price index, the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge, rose in August, but not by as much as spending increased, suggesting that inflation was not a major headwind for U.S. consumers.

Traders bet the Fed would be comfortable continuing its rate-cut cycle with inflation at the current level, weighing on the value of the U.S. dollar.

Gold futures, which are particularly sensitive to dollar moves, closed at $3775.30 an ounce, the second highest level ever.

Wheat futures fell on the Chicago Board of Trade after a bumper harvest in the U.S.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Industrials Up as Boeing Fears Fade - Industrials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of industrial and transportation companies rose amid signs that Boeing was putting safety and engineering woes behind it.

Shares of the aerospace giant added to recent gains after the Federal Aviation Administration said it was ceding authority to perform final safety checks on 737 MAX jets to the company starting next week.

Shares of heavy-duty truck maker Paccar rose after the Trump administration said it would impose tariffs on heavy truck imports.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Health Care Cos Up After Trump Tariff Threat - Health Care Roundup
data.description:
Health-care companies rose after the Trump administration's long threatened pharmaceutical tariffs were not as draconian as some had feared.

The U.S. will impose 100% duties on branded pharmaceutical imports, unless the manufacturer has a U.S. factory or is building one.

Biotech giant Amgen said it would invest $650 million in expanding its U.S manufacturing network, a day after President Trump said he would impose a 100% tariff on pharmaceuticals produced by companies that are not building plants in the U.S.

Dentalcorp Holdings has agreed to be acquired by buyout shop GTCR in a deal that values the network of dental practices at about $1.58 billion.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Financials Up Amid Deal Boom - Financials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of banks and other financial institutions rose as a deal and speculative boom continued.

A consortium led by tech investment firm Silver Lake and Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund has approached videogame maker Electronic Arts with a leveraged-buyout offer worth around $50 billion, The Wall Street Journal reported.

Corporations and investors are showing a greater willingness to borrow money as the Federal Reserve cuts rates.

"A soft jobs report could very well shift the balance of risks in favor of pulling forward the spaced-out rate cuts we envision in 2026 (in March and June) after the consecutive cuts we expect at the next two meetings," said economists at brokerage BNP Paribas.

Former financier Howard Rubin was charged Friday with trafficking dozens of women whom federal prosecutors said he sexually and physically assaulted during encounters in a Central Park penthouse with a sex dungeon.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Consumer Cos Up After Strong Consumer Spending Data - Consumer Roundup
data.description:
Consumer companies rose after surprisingly strong spending growth.

Consumer spending rose 0.6% in August, exceeding economists' expectations. The PCE index, the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge, rose 0.3% in August, data that is likely to reassure top officials at the Fed that inflation is still relatively well contained despite tariff impacts.

"We believe the FOMC meeting and recent Fed communications show a shift in the Fed's reaction function, putting more weight on downside risks to the labor market and a lower emphasis on still-elevated inflation," said economists at brokerage BNP Paribas.

Consumer confidence waned in September, according to the latest survey from the University of Michigan.

Activist investor Land & Buildings Investment Management is pressuring Six Flags Entertainment to spin out or sell its real estate in an effort to revive the theme-park operator's languishing share price.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Communications Services Up on Risk Appetite - Communications Services Roundup
data.description:
Communications-services companies rose as traders sought out higher risk areas.

Local television broadcaster Sinclair is putting Jimmy Kimmel back on the air, ending a preemption of the "Jimmy Kimmel Live!" show in markets where it owns ABC affiliates.

British users of popular social-media sites Facebook and Instagram will have the option to pay for an advertisement-free service, the sites' owner Meta Platforms said.

Details on the acquisition of the U.S. arm of TikTok, including the percentage of profits Chinese owner ByteDance will retain could explain the relatively low $14 billion valuation for the popular social-media app disclosed by President Trump, analysts said.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: First Lithium Minerals Corp Completes Flow-Through Financing
data.description:
Toronto, Ontario--(Newsfile Corp. - September 26, 2025) - First Lithium Minerals Corp.  (OTC Pink: FLMCF) (FSE: X28) ("First Lithium Minerals" or the "Company") announces that it has completed its previously announced flow-through financing for gross proceeds of $75,000. The Company issued 937,500 flow-through shares (the "Flow-Through Shares") at an issue price of $0.08 per share.

Finders fees were paid totalling $2,450 of cash and 30,625 finders warrants, with each finder warrant exercisable into a common share at an exercise price of $0.08 per share for 18 months.

All securities issued under the offering will be subject to a hold period expiring four months and one day from the date of the closing.

Insiders of the Company purchased 500,000 Flow-Through Shares. The participation by the insider constitutes a "related party transaction" within the meaning of the policies of the CSE and Multilateral Instrument 61-101 - Protection of Minority Security Holders in Special Transactions ("MI 61-101"). The Company is relying upon the exemptions from the formal valuation and minority shareholder approval requirements pursuant to sections 5.5(a) and (b), and 5.7(1)(a), respectively, of MI 61-101 on the basis that neither the fair market value of the subject matter of, nor the fair market value of the consideration for, the transaction insofar as it involves interested parties (within the meaning of MI 61-101) in the financing exceeds 25% of the Company's market capitalization calculated in accordance with MI 61-101, and on the basis that no securities of the Company are listed or quoted on a stock exchange as specified in MI 61-101.

About First Lithium Minerals

First Lithium Minerals is a Canadian mineral exploration and development company. The Company is exploring for lithium and alkali metals at its 100% owned Ascotan Project comprised of approximately 1,775 ha of mineral exploration concessions located at the Salar de Ascotan in the Antofagasta Region of northern Chile. Two property-wide geophysical surveys identified priority exploration drill targets for potential brine mineralization. The Company is currently planning its inaugural drilling program pending obtaining required drilling permits, licences and agreements. First Lithium Minerals is also exploring for gold and critical metals at its 100% owned Lidstone project comprised of 17,300 ha of mining claims in northwestern Ontario, Canada.

Additional information about the Company is available on the Company's website: www.firstlithium.ca

For further information, please contact:

First Lithium Minerals Corp.

40 King Street West, Suite 2400

Toronto, Ontario

M5H 3Y2

Tel: 416-402-2428

Email: rob@firstlithium.ca

Rob Saltsman, CEO and Director

Caution Regarding Forward-Looking Statements

This news release contains "forward-looking information" within the meaning of applicable securities laws. Any such forward-looking information may be identified by words such as "expects", "anticipates", "intends", "contemplates", "believes", "projects", "plans", and similar expressions. data.title: Iran's President Defiant as Sanctions Set to Return — WSJ
data.description:
By Michael R. Gordon, Laurence Norman and Robbie Gramer

Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian delivered a defiant response Friday, as Western nations move to reimpose international sanctions on Tehran for failing to uphold the 2015 nuclear deal.

Pezeshkian said that Iran wouldn't quit the Non-Proliferation Treaty, which commits members to not build nuclear weapons. But he displayed little flexibility on the core nuclear issues that have divided Tehran and the U.S. and signaled no movement toward the U.S. demand that Iran cease enriching uranium.

Nor did he say whether international inspectors might soon visit key Iranian nuclear sites, as the International Atomic Energy Agency has urged.

Pezeshkian spoke in New York as the United Nations Security Council voted down a Russian resolution which would have delayed the reimposition of the sanctions — dubbed "snapback" by international diplomats — that are set to go into effect early Sunday.

The reestablishment of the sanctions is "unfair, unjust and illegal," said Pezeshkian. "The wall of mistrust that has been created between us and the Americans is quite thick and quite high."

The reimposition of sanctions marks the collapse of the 2015 nuclear deal, which offered relief from the punitive economic measures in exchange for strict but temporary limits on its nuclear work.

The U.S. quit the agreement in 2018 under President Trump. A year later, Tehran started to exceed the limits the agreement imposed on its nuclear activities.

The return of economic, military and nuclear sanctions this weekend is a fresh blow to Iran's beleaguered economy.

There will be repercussions for the U.S. and its allies, too. Tehran has threatened to retaliate by locking out international inspectors who want to account for Iran's fissile material stockpile and visit the key nuclear sites that were damaged by the Israeli and U.S. airstrikes in June. Tehran is also vowing to continue its nuclear work.

Without inspectors on the ground, the U.S., Israel and its allies will need to rely on satellite imagery and intelligence to keep tabs on — and potentially respond with force to — Tehran's nuclear work.

Israel has threatened to act militarily if Iran seeks to rebuild its program. Before the June attacks, Iran had produced enough highly enriched uranium to fuel almost 10 nuclear weapons.

That has left the two sides in an uneasy standoff with the U.S. and its allies watching from afar as Iran looks to rebuild its badly battered military and nuclear capabilities.

For Iran's stumbling economy, which is facing high inflation and is contracting for the first time in four years, the return of international sanctions is a new blow. Iran's currency weakened to near record lows against the dollar ahead of snapback, pushing up prices.

U.S. and European officials hope that the renewed economic and diplomatic pressure on Iran, together with Iran's military vulnerabilities, will eventually force Tehran back to the negotiating table.

After the U.S. and Israel bombed Iran's nuclear sites in June, the Pentagon said that the airstrikes had set back Tehran's nuclear program for up to two years, a more modest claim than President Trump's initial assertion that the military action had "obliterated" Iran's nuclear efforts.

Vice President JD Vance indicated soon after the U.S. attack that Iran still possessed some caches of enriched uranium, whose disposition he hoped would be the subject of future talks.

But negotiations between Tehran and Washington haven't yet resumed. The Trump administration wants swift and direct talks with Iran to resolve the nuclear file. Iran has so far agreed only to multilateral talks and has sought guarantees it won't be attacked.

The economic impact, however, may not be enough to force Tehran to change course. Trump's decision to reimpose U.S. sanctions on Tehran in 2018 swiftly froze Iran's trade with Europe and many other developed economies because of the threat of hefty U.S. penalties on foreign companies.

Bijan Khajehpour, Iran analyst and managing partner of the Eurasian Nexus Partners business-consulting firm, said he expects the currency to come under further pressure after snapback, with inflation lifting from around 40% to 50%.

He said the biggest economic impact could come for Iranian exporters who could find themselves cut off from regional companies providing crucial services. That includes money exchanges that help Iranian firms repatriate export revenues and regional insurance and transport firms.

"I think there will be some real practical impact, especially for companies that don't have the protection of the Iranian government," he said.

Write to Michael R. Gordon at michael.gordon@wsj.com, Laurence Norman at laurence.norman@wsj.com and Robbie Gramer at robbie.gramer@wsj.com

data.title: Uber's $12.5 Billion Bet: Can Delivery Overtake Ridesharing?
data.description:
Uber Technologies Inc. is dialing up expectations in grocery and retail delivery, now guiding to a $12.5 billion annual run rate in gross bookings by the end of 2025. That forecast represents a 25% increase over the $10 billion run rate the company had shared just months earlier, pointing to stronger-than-anticipated adoption. CEO Dara Khosrowshahi has described delivery as a key growth driver, noting that it has outpaced the rideshare unit for three consecutive quarters. He also emphasized that the majority of Uber's rideshare customers have yet to try grocery and retail ordering, underscoring what he sees as a long adoption runway.

The competitive landscape remains crowded, with Instacart, DoorDash, Amazon and Walmart all vying for share, but Uber believes its platform integration could help it capture incremental demand. Current usage patterns suggest most orders are last-minute or urgent, with peak activity on Sunday evenings for basic staples like bananas, avocados and tomatoes. Executives suggest this highlights a significant opportunity to shift consumer behavior toward recurring, planned purchases, which could expand the business beyond convenience-driven use cases.

To build that habit, Uber has added over a thousand new retail partners in 2025, including Aldi, Sephora, Best Buy, Dollar General and Dollar Tree, broadening its retail footprint. The company also introduced weekly discounts across fresh food categories in multiple countries and rolled out features like live order editing and improved substitution tools. Global delivery head Susan Anderson is expected to expand on these developments during her keynote at the Groceryshop conference in Las Vegas, an event that could provide investors with fresh insight into whether delivery can continue to accelerate as a growth engine alongside ridesharing.

data.title: Sayward Capital Corp. Announces Resumption of Trading on the TSX Venture Exchange
data.description:
Calgary, Alberta--(Newsfile Corp. - September 26, 2025) - Sayward Capital Corp. (the "Corporation") (TSXV: SAWC.P), a capital pool company listed on the TSX Venture Exchange ("TSXV" or the "Exchange"), is pleased to report that following a successful resumption review by the TSXV, the Corporation's common shares are expected to resume trading on the Exchange on September 30, 2025.

For further information, please contact:

Sayward Capital Corp.

Sayward Capital Corp.

data.title: Lockheed Martin's Sikorsky secures about $11 billion US navy contract
data.description:
Lockheed Martin said on Friday its subsidiary, Sikorsky, has secured a nearly $11 billion contract from the U.S. Navy to build up to 99 CH-53K King Stallion helicopters.

It is the largest order to date for the aircraft, the company said.

The five-year contract will allow the U.S. government to buy up to 99 CH-53K aircraft for the Marine Corps or to fulfill orders from international military customers.
